summary:
Market Analysis Update: March 26, 2026

The Canadian bond market remains in a state of "bear flattening" as of March 26, 2026, driven by a hawkish overnight sentiment from the U.S. Federal Reserve and persistent energy-driven inflation concerns. Following the March 24th U.S. Treasury volatility, the Government of Canada 5-year yield has held firm at 3.20%, while the 2-year yield remains elevated at 2.99%. This narrow 21-basis-point spread reflects a market pricing in a "higher-for-longer" scenario, despite February’s domestic CPI cooling to 1.8%. The tight correlation between U.S. and Canadian yields continues to override domestic fundamentals, as investors demand a higher term premium to account for geopolitical instability in the Middle East and the Bank of Canada’s recent warnings regarding upside risks to the 2.25% policy rate.

For our institution, this environment has created a critical margin squeeze. While our best 5-year fixed mortgage rate has been held at 3.94% to maintain market share, the underlying 5-year swap and bond yields have surged, compressing our lending margin to a thin 0.74%—down significantly from the ~1.00% levels seen earlier this month. Simultaneously, the rise in the 2-year yield to nearly 3.00% is aggressively driving up our cost of funds. To protect institutional profitability and maintain liquidity, we must consider an immediate upward adjustment to our fixed-rate mortgage pricing to restore historical margins. Furthermore, we face the challenge of raising GIC and term deposit rates to remain competitive, which will further pressure net interest margins (NIM) unless the yield curve begins to steepen or yields retreat from these multi-month highs.
